inline_comments.message: this is ok for the moment - its remotely possible to get "Not Applicable" but I would hope not.

Should "Not applicable" be treated as success, or as failure?  Perhaps I should define a set of success values and a set of failure values and then just do a look-up instead of using equals() comparison.  Is there a place where the possible values are defined?  At this point, anything it doesn't recognize is treated as a "DENY".

In my opinion, "Not applicable" means we screwed up in writing the policies. More along the lines of a bug. In production we should never get NA, but it is remotely possible to get Indeterminate - eg. the request may be missing attributes. Could be also considered a "bug".

What we have been doing is defaulting any other than Permit is a Deny. Just to be safe. And best if we can propogate details as to why (but we need to pass that information from xacml pdp back - another Pam TODO) so someone can either push or modify a guard policy that remedies the situation.
